title: JavaScript variables and console.log()
summary: Learn how to save values in JavaScript and display them with console.log().
goal: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to create simple variables and print their values in JavaScript.
explanation: In JavaScript, a variable stores data so you can use it later. This helps your code stay organized and reusable.
console.log() is a simple tool that prints information to the browser console. Developers often use it to test what their code is doing.
When you store values in variables, you can change them in one place instead of rewriting them everywhere.
codeNotes: const creates a variable whose value should not be reassigned later.
console.log() helps you inspect values while learning or debugging.
The final console.log() combines text and variable values into one readable message.
commonMistakes: Forgetting semicolons if your project style expects them consistently.
Trying to use a variable name that was never declared.

exercise.title: Introduce a learner
exercise.instructions: Create variables for a learner name and a favorite topic. Then use console.log() to print a sentence that introduces the learner and topic.

title: JavaScript if statements
summary: Learn how JavaScript checks a condition before running some code.
goal: By the end of this lesson, you will be able to write a simple if statement in JavaScript.
explanation: An if statement gives your program a choice. It checks whether a condition is true.
If the condition is true, the code inside the curly braces runs. If it is false, that block is skipped.
This is one of the first steps toward making interactive programs that react to user actions or data.
codeNotes: score >= 70 returns true because 92 is greater than 70.
Because the condition is true, the message inside the curly braces runs.
The last console.log() runs whether the condition is true or false.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the curly braces around the code block.
Using = instead of === or another comparison operator.
Mixing up greater than > and greater than or equal to >=.

exercise.title: Check lesson progress
exercise.instructions: Create a variable called completedLessons. If the value is 3 or more, print a message that says the learner can move to the next lesson.
